description.task: Create a table with NOT NULL and CHECK constraints, then test both valid and invalid data to observe constraint enforcement.
instructions: Run the CREATE TABLE and all three valid INSERT statements.
Run the SELECT to confirm 3 rows — Carol's department should be 'General' (default).
Uncomment the NULL first_name insert — run it and read the error.
Re-comment and uncomment the age=16 insert — run and read the error.
Add a comment: why is NOT NULL different from an empty string ('')?
description.hint: NULL means 'no value / unknown'. An empty string '' is a valid value that happens to contain no characters. A NOT NULL column can still store ''.
description.expectedOutput:
3 valid rows: Carol's department = 'General'
NULL insert: 'null value in column violates not-null constraint'
Age=16 insert: 'new row violates check constraint'

description.task: Use UNIQUE constraints to prevent duplicate values and DEFAULT constraints to auto-fill standard values.
instructions: Run CREATE TABLE and all INSERT statements.
Run the first SELECT — note that alice and bob got DEFAULT role='viewer', is_active=TRUE, login_count=0.
Run the UPDATE statements and second SELECT.
Uncomment the duplicate username insert — read the error message.
In a comment: can a table have multiple UNIQUE constraints? Multiple NOT NULL constraints?
description.hint: A table can have as many UNIQUE and NOT NULL constraints as needed — there's no limit. Only PRIMARY KEY is limited to one per table.
description.expectedOutput:
After inserts: alice/viewer, bob/viewer, carol/admin — all active with login_count=0
After updates: alice login_count=1, carol=5
Duplicate username error on 'alice'

title: FOREIGN KEY with ON DELETE Actions

description.task: Demonstrate the three FOREIGN KEY deletion behaviours: RESTRICT, CASCADE, and SET NULL.
description.context: When a parent row is deleted, the FK constraint determines what happens to child rows. RESTRICT blocks the delete, CASCADE deletes children too, and SET NULL nullifies the FK column.
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Observe: employees_setnull shows Alice and Carol with NULL dept_id; employees_cascade shows only Eve.
Add a comment: when would you choose each deletion behaviour (SET NULL, CASCADE, RESTRICT)?
description.hint: SET NULL: useful when an employee might exist without a department. CASCADE: useful for a blog where deleting a post also deletes its comments. RESTRICT (default): safest — prevents accidental cascading deletes.
description.expectedOutput:
employees_setnull: Alice(dept_id=NULL), Bob(dept_id=2), Carol(dept_id=NULL)
employees_cascade: Eve(dept_id=2) only — Dave and Frank were cascade deleted
